speaker: interviewer
text: OK, today's date is February 5, 1997. The survivor we're interviewing is Ilona Fuchs. Her maiden name Engel. Interviewer is Cathy Renner. The city is Los Angeles in the state of California in the United States. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Cathy Renner, R-E-N-N-E-R. Today is February 5, 1997. I'm conducting an interview with Mrs. Ilona Fuchs in Pasadena, California, United States of America. The language, English. Good afternoon. Could you please give me your name and the spelling of the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ilona Fuchs. And my maiden name is Engel. Ilona, I-L-O-N-A, F-U-C-H-S. My maiden name, E-N-G-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nickname Hungarian-- Itsa.

speaker: interviewer
text: When is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1918, March 31st.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to be, next month, 79.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city and country were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tokaj T-O-K-A-J or Y. In Zemplen county, in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell me who are the members of your family and what they were like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents died at the Holocaust. My mother's name was Flora or Laura Engel, but the maiden name Szasz, S-Z-A-S-Z. And my father name, Salomon Engel-- Engel the last name. Both born in Tokaj, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell me a little bit about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: As child I couldn't have a nicer parents. They were very kind, very nice. We lived in, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I should say, Gentile neighborhood. But we was very good friends. My father was in the first war. And neighbors, not everybody, had a good water in the premises. We have natural well. We don't have the faucet like here in America, in the house where I born. And people, the neighbor came over for drinking water who doesn't have the well. But many people drink it from the bank of the river, the Tisza, because we lived in the bank of the river.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had five sisters. Two older sisters came to America 1937. The oldest one is Renea, R-E-N-E-A. And the second one, Margit, M-A-R-G-I-T. The third one Erzsabet-- or Elizabeth in America. The fourth one, me, Ilona, or how Morton called me, Ilonka. And the youngest one is Charlotta, America, Charlotte.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your relation with your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful. We don't see each 20 years, because they came '37. And we-- when we escaped, we arrived '57.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war--

speaker: interviewer
text: --what was your relation with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the fourth one. So the oldest one, you know, played with us and came to see the play in the school. It's a very close relationship was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you closer to any one of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, after the Holocaust, we three of us, you know, Elizabeth, and Charlotte, and me, we are much closer. However, my two older sisters now live here in Pasadena. The oldest one is at a retirement home. And my other sister, I close, but I cannot talk to them about  the Holocaust and what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living? Your family in general?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother, father, parents, have a [NON-ENGLISH]-- a tavern, I think called in English. And my father, when married, came to same house. Both my father, here called real estate-- selling the barnyard and the houses. It's a different way, most of the time. Tokaj is famous for the wine, and a lot of grape mountain in this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe the tavern?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a very familiar tavern, not like here, the bars. People have regular way to came. And after work, or after supper at home, they came over and play card-- you know, not-- not like in Las Vegas card. And the little children, even, watched that. And my mother-- they respected her. If anybody would come who was drunk, she don't serve. Or we have some help her. And how much I heard, the house my grandfather-- who I never know, this side, my mother's side grandfather-- built it, 1856. Where all the five children were born, and even my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have grandparents that you were close to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only have grandfather, and his second wife, very close. Like he had many-- before the war I'm talking about-- he had many grandchildren. But for Seder, he always wanted me. Even when I was in a bigger city, after when I got the diploma and needed for [INAUDIBLE], came home for Passover, my mother said, Zayde said he would like to you go for Seder. And only alone. And I never had met the first wife. The second wife, Bobbi, was-- one hand, you know, couldn't move. Very close, we was. I was there a couple hours, even when he gets sick, it was custom was the big synagogue, you saw. And they have a [NON-ENGLISH], what-- what some help to put the new roof from his gran. And he went Sunday, and came home. And I supposed to help my uncle at the office, some office work. And he came and he said, Zayde want to, you stay with him. So I was there till-- if somebody want to have a-- die quietly like that-- this was Sunday. And he said he want to have-- to see all the children, and to live in Bratislava, Czechoslovakia, and [NON-ENGLISH] of the youngest daughter and family. And once they-- everybody was there, he blessed them, even my two older sisters at that time. And I had an uncle, the oldest one, who was very religious, and he said to his father to tell Renea and Margit then blessed, and then they should wear a shawl, too. He said, no, I just want to they should marry a Jewish boy. And they did. He was in those age, a very modern-thinking gentleman. This picture in our dining room, the reason we bought it, because it looked like exactly my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? Moshe Engel-- Engel Mor, M-O-R, E-N-G-E-L, And Hebrew name Moshe [NON-ENGLISH]. And what are your memories of the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure-- my mother, the nicest holiday-- Shavuos. I told you when-- and-- oh, I told you we lived in a Gentile neighborhood. When was a special Catholic holiday, in front of the Catholic church they put flowers. So my mother, we had a big backyard and garden. But when Erev Shavuos, they know that Flora, how they called my mother, going to make the wreath, the crown to the Torah. So we have lots of flowers. And with the children to get to the temple. After the Holocaust, unfortunately, my parents didn't come back. But my younger sister and me, we did it in Tokaj. And Henry, and Martha, and my niece were [INAUDIBLE] reach the temple. And now a couple years ago, here in Pasadena, a young lady came over to ask me what kind custom was in Shavuos, and I told them. And that's going to be the 40 or 30 years that we making the crown to the Torah. Beautiful holiday. For me, is a yahrzeit. But I try to, when I make the flower, I try to remember the happy time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there another holiday that you would like to tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover. And we always have whoever came to Tokaj. And even when, after the Jewish law, when we went to the apartment in Tokaj. And it was already 1939 when we moved there. And they have Jewish boy that time in the army. My father always came home to three people, because there was a custom before the Seder, they went to the synagogue. He had service, and we never know he came home, two people, or three people. But-- That's the way the life was. And it was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it that you liked the best about Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: To get together the family and celebrate the freedom and think about [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] no matter how the time was. And was hard to get the food. But always was in Passover, the chicken soup and the fish and the matzah. And a nice to open up the door for Elijah. And-- and wishing yourself and the whole family some good things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your home life, your daily life like at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even the hardest years at home, we was very close. And no fighting or misunderstanding about the family. Is needed to work for the every day living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: In high holidays. In those days the girls doesn't went to the synagogue like here. And elementary school, I went to public school. And the third grade, actually Morton's uncle, was the parochial school principal. And he, now and then, we tend to go-- you know, I was about 6 year or 7, I started five years to school. And he talked to our principal then, twice, that we needed to go the-- next to the synagogue at the Jewish school, to religious education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe your religious education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish education? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We learned-- my mother hired in summer a yeshiva bocher to teach us the Hebrew letters before we went. And we learned some prayers and the history. And then I was in high school. That's also was a public high school, a girls' school at that time. And they came, all the teachers, one times a week. And for the three grade, we was together-- about four or five Jewish girls, and taught us the prayers, and most of them the history of the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the public school, what was the relationship between the Jews and the non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wasn't no problem. In-- in-- really, till 1939 or something, when they start, when Hitler came to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in Tokaj and how many were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only things I remember, approximately at that time, 5,000. But the ghetto, we were 1,200. Without Morton, my husband, I don't know how many hundred went to the labor camp. I don't know. But most of the businesses at the was Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish community like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big Jewish community. There was an orthodox [NON-ENGLISH]. And they have sisterhood, and-- my mother belong at only one things, I remember, after when my two sisters left. And they had, before, I think Purim, nice going to a big event, and have-- it is the hot dog they called [NON-ENGLISH] evening, and was some music. And we hardly waited then the [NON-ENGLISH] to leave, then we can dance.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian. My parents speak German. But because I told we had the neighbor who was not Jewish people, so I never learned Yiddish. And I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of literature did you have at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of-- what did you read at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, novels, good books. And we went to the movie that was the less expensive entertainment. And most of the time they came to the city some theater group, and magician group, and circus. And, you know, we went to ice skating, and some concert.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you celebrate your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, in Hungary, if I could remember, maybe among the very close family. But we celebrate name day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like you say it, Ilona, because people know your name, so that's your remember. They don't know your birthday. Except your sister, or mother, or father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you celebrate that?

speaker: interviewee
text: People bring some flowers. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and there was and nice custom, I'm talking about before the war, like May 1st, we had five girls and one boyfriend or good friend. And they get the gypsy, sometimes not gypsy, and come to your window and play the violin, a nice serenade. Sometimes I tell my children that you people miss that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any Jewish organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't have it. If I could remember, just before we moved, I tried to remember came from Vienna or something-- a girl. I don't know how they called-- and some young girls, they get together, I cannot remember the name. But after the war I belonged to the [NON-ENGLISH]-- a couple them. But we needed to burn the papers as  soon as the communists heard about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before the war was your family involved in politics?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family spend the free time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went to visit the relatives. We lived about three miles from the city. And all week, there was a six days a week working day. And Friday night, especially winter time, you know, my father went to the synagogue and came home. And after supper, oh let's go to Uncle [NON-ENGLISH], or let's go in [NON-ENGLISH], where lived one of my mother's side uncle lived, and also a cousin. So walking distance. And we visited each other. And we girls visited each other, and talked about it or read about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You kept kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you get your food?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tokaj, this small city was two kosher butchers. And they have a open market. And my mother bought the chicken and was a shochet who had to kill the chicken. And was a woman up there who cleaned from the feather. And they-- that's we learned, how you make kosher a chicken. But we don't have to do that here, because you buy kosher meat, already kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a mikvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. It was beautiful. You know-- you can go in wintertime with your mother and you can swim in that. Even today, I think they still prepare the mikvah. The mikvah was big like this house. And they were separate, I don't know how many little rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then was a big-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my goodness-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] tub, where the women stayed first [? at bath ?], and washed their hair. And there was a mikvah maven, a lady-- and you go out and three times see you go under the water. And she say, [NON-ENGLISH]. And always, my mother took one of us. And this was a-- a special occasion.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the mikvah, even after the Holocaust, they was working. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They don't have a swimming pool. We have the Tisza, the big river to go to swim, but not in winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Tokaj like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tokaj is a very beautiful little city. Two river and a big mountain. So really, they have a bit in the mountain and river in the city. But where we lived, bank of the river, there was a-- a bigger place to build. So-- that's-- and we have in Tokaj two or three stone-- not mill, how they call-- you know, where they took out the stone for building the house. And have a mill where they take the wheat and make flour. And a museum, and have the Catholic church, the evangelic church, the reform church, and the Greek Catholic, and the Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if I could describe, is a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a well-educated people was there. And friendly people. For our son-- he travel a lot, and giving lecture. But for him, the relaxation in Tokaj. And after you graduated from high school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: After you graduated from high school, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I needed to wait almost a year. I was too young. And I want to be always a teacher. And only one Jewish college was in Hungary. And because I graduated public school, I needed to make a special test to pass it. And they accepted me, but people already came the law that if I go into-- that was after high school, was a four year-- four year or third year, I don't remember, to get the pay, as like here. Then, I couldn't get a job, because I'm a Jew. Because our father was hurt and got the medal from the first war, so the high school education till then we was good grade was free for us. And I could get state university free, the Jewish university cost money. But they said if I finish, I couldn't teach, because I'm Jewish. So I was-- think about a very serious girl. I don't even know how they thread the needle, but I asked my sister, who was living in Pasadena, maybe I should learn to designing dresses, and dress-making, and then I will teach.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in a private [NON-ENGLISH], apprentice, a private college, a private person, who had three or four students-- two years, I went, five days a week, six days many times.

speaker: interviewee
text: I get the diploma. but you couldn't open up a designing place or teaching-- you needed to have two years really working to get apprenticeship. And then after came the Jewish law-- I couldn't do it. Only just go to the houses. Because for working, like a day work, like a cleaning woman-- you could [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So it doesn't make difference if I went to the house, and I designed for the ladies the dresses. And most of the time I have a book-- two days this place, three days this place. And it was nice remembering when, in maybe about 10 months, they send the thing for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And once my younger sister came to help, two horse, a beautiful horse, with-- but I don't know how they call this, the fancy carriage-- and wintertime they even send that to one coat. And I stayed there from Sunday till Friday.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are going to change tapes now.
